topic: Someone receives flowers with no card attached
raw_output:
[story 1]

The bouquet was a riot of deep crimson roses, their velvet petals cool against my fingertips. I stood by the entryway, the silence of the apartment amplifying the slight, frantic thumping in my chest. There was no note. No signature. Just the vibrant, almost aggressive beauty of the blooms resting on the polished wood of the hall table. I traced the edge of a petal, my gaze darting toward the door, half-expecting someone to materialize, to demand an explanation for this unsolicited offering. My breath hitched, a shallow, quick intake of air. I didn't move to take them, just stared, a knot tightening low in my stomach.

[story 2]

I found the arrangement tucked beside my laptop on the kitchen counter. Yellow lilies, impossibly perfect, seemed to mock the ordinary clutter of my morning routine. I picked one up. The stem felt strangely stiff, almost brittle. Every instinct screamed at me to examine the wrapping paper, to search for a hidden message, but the paper was pristine, unmarked. I turned the flower over in my hand, the fragrance heavy and cloying in the air. I paced the length of the living room three times, my footsteps echoing too loudly on the hardwood floor. I kept glancing toward the window, wondering if the sunlight was behaving strangely, or if I was simply misinterpreting the weight of this gesture.

[story 3]

When the delivery person knocked, I opened the door expecting a package, not a cascade of pale blue irises. They were wrapped in thick, cream-colored paper, tied with a simple, uneven twine. As I took them, a tremor ran up my arm, a sudden, sharp awareness of being observed. I looked down at the flowers, then up at the figure standing on my porch—a silhouette against the afternoon glare. I felt a peculiar pressure behind my eyes, a need to ask a question that my throat refused to form. I clutched the flowers tightly against my chest, the coldness of the paper seeping into my palms, and backed slowly into the hallway, seeking refuge behind the closed door.
